name: Quantization
oneliner: Converting a model's high-precision floats to lower-precision representations — the single most impactful optimization for LLM serving.
explanation: Quantization maps model weights from their original 16-bit or 32-bit format to smaller representations like 8-bit, 4-bit, or even lower. This matters because LLM inference is memory-bandwidth-bound: generating each token requires reading every weight from memory, so shrinking the weights directly speeds up generation. A 70-billion parameter model in 16-bit takes about 140 GB; in 4-bit it fits in roughly 35 GB. Quality impact varies by precision: 8-bit is essentially lossless, 4-bit adds small but measurable degradation, and below 4-bit requires specialized techniques.
fundamentals: Uniform affine quantization: $x_q = \text{clamp}(\text{round}(x/s + z),\, q_{\min},\, q_{\max})$. Dequantize: $\hat{x} = s \cdot (x_q - z)$. Scale $s = (x_{\max} - x_{\min})/(q_{\max} - q_{\min})$. Symmetric: $z=0$, $s = \max(|x|)/q_{\max}$. Error per value bounded by $s/2$. Not all weights contribute equally — attention layers and first/last layers are typically more sensitive.
sources: Gholami et al., 'A Survey of Quantization Methods,' arXiv:2103.13630

foundational_papers.title: A Survey of Quantization Methods for Efficient Neural Network Inference
